name: Figure AI
description: Figure AI is the best-funded pure-play humanoid robotics company in the world, having raised over $2.25B through its Series C in early 2026. Founded by serial entrepreneur Brett Adcock, the company is vertically integrated across hardware, actuators, and its proprietary Helix vision-language-action model. Figure's BMW partnership — now expanded to four plants — represents the most operationally significant humanoid deployment outside of Tesla's internal program.

name: Tesla (Optimus Division)
description: Tesla's Optimus division operates as an internal business unit within Tesla, Inc., drawing on the company's Dojo supercomputer, FSD neural net stack, and Gigafactory manufacturing infrastructure. With approximately 8,000 units deployed internally across Giga Texas and Giga Shanghai, Tesla has more humanoid robots operational than any other entity globally. Tesla's structural advantage — owning its training compute, manufacturing, and deployment environment — creates a data flywheel effect that pure-play competitors cannot easily replicate.

name: Boston Dynamics
ceo: Robert Playter (stepped down Mar 2026; interim leadership)
description: Boston Dynamics is the world's most technically credentialed robotics company, with over three decades of DARPA-funded research producing iconic platforms including BigDog, Spot, and Atlas. Acquired by Hyundai Motor Group in 2021 for approximately $1.1B, Boston Dynamics now operates as a Hyundai subsidiary with Spot generating meaningful commercial revenue and Atlas Electric in advanced R&D. The company's shift from hydraulic to electric actuation for Atlas signals a deliberate move toward manufacturable, commercially viable humanoids aligned with Hyundai's factory automation ambitions.

name: Agility Robotics
description: Agility Robotics spun out of Oregon State University's Dynamic Robotics Laboratory, where founder Jonathan Hurst developed the Cassie bipedal research platform. The company's Digit robot is one of only two humanoids (alongside Tesla Optimus) with demonstrated large-scale commercial deployment, operating in Amazon fulfillment centers. CEO Peggy Johnson, former Microsoft EVP and Qualcomm executive, joined in 2023 to lead the commercialization push; Amazon's participation as lead Series B investor provides both capital and the world's largest warehouse deployment network.

name: 1X Technologies
description: 1X Technologies (formerly Halodi Robotics) is Norway's leading humanoid robotics company, distinguished by its safety-first design philosophy and long-term focus on home assistant applications. The company's EVE wheeled robot has been commercially deployed in security and logistics roles, providing operational revenue that few humanoid-focused startups can claim. Backed by OpenAI's startup fund in its 2023 Series A, 1X is developing NEO as its bipedal home-assistant platform through an unusually methodical, data-driven rollout process.

name: Sanctuary AI
description: Sanctuary AI is founded by Geordie Rose, co-founder of quantum computing company D-Wave, and pursues one of the most ambitious cognitive architectures in humanoid robotics through its Carbon AI system. Carbon is designed to reason about the physical world using a generative model of task semantics rather than relying solely on learned sensorimotor policies. The company's Canadian Tire retail deployment represents one of the few unscripted, real-world humanoid service deployments outside of a controlled factory environment.

name: Apptronik
description: Apptronik emerged from the Human Centered Robotics Lab at UT Austin and has deep NASA ties through development of the Valkyrie robot and Space Exploration Suit actuators. This heritage gives Apollo's actuator technology exceptional force control fidelity and reliability benchmarks established through space-qualified hardware programs. Google's lead investment in the $350M Series A in late 2024, combined with an early 2026 500-unit commercial contract, positions Apptronik as the most commercially advanced American humanoid company outside of Tesla and Figure AI.

name: Unitree Robotics
description: Unitree Robotics has disrupted the global robotics market with aggressive price-performance positioning across quadruped and humanoid platforms. The Go1 and Go2 quadrupeds established Unitree as the volume leader in legged robotics, and the $16,000 H1 humanoid extended that strategy to bipedal platforms. Unitree's manufacturing efficiency — enabled by vertical integration of motor, gearbox, and controller production — creates a cost structure that Western competitors cannot match at equivalent margin.

name: NEURA Robotics
description: NEURA Robotics is Europe's most-funded pure-play humanoid startup, pursuing a 'cognitive humanoid' philosophy that integrates multi-modal sensing with high-level reasoning to produce robots capable of operating in unstructured environments without extensive prior programming. CEO David Reger, a veteran of the German automotive robotics industry, has positioned NEURA at the intersection of European engineering precision and AI-native robot design. The company's German base provides natural access to BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and Bosch as development partners, all of whom face acute skilled labor shortages.

name: Fourier Intelligence
description: Fourier Intelligence is distinguished among humanoid companies by its decade-long commercial track record in medical exoskeletons, having shipped thousands of rehabilitation devices globally before pivoting to humanoids. This background provides mature actuator technology, medical-grade quality systems, and an existing revenue base. The GR-1 and GR2 platforms leverage exoskeleton actuator heritage to offer a compelling combination of human-proportioned morphology and demonstrated manufacturing capability.

name: Agibot
description: Agibot is one of China's fastest-growing humanoid startups, founded in 2023 with backing from prominent Chinese venture funds and state capital. The company's core differentiation is its large-scale teleoperation data collection infrastructure — Agibot operates one of China's largest fleets of teleoperated robots for building proprietary manipulation training datasets. This data-centric strategy positions Agibot to benefit disproportionately as foundation models for robotics mature.

name: Kepler
description: Kepler differentiates in a crowded Chinese humanoid market by targeting the highest-payload segment of factory automation, with the Forerunner offering 35kg capacity and 8-hour runtime that no competitor matches. The company's Suzhou manufacturing base, within China's premier precision manufacturing cluster, provides access to high-quality component suppliers and automotive customers. Kepler has focused exclusively on factory environments where economics are clearest and safety cases are most tractable.

name: Clone Robotics
description: Clone Robotics is a bootstrapped Polish company pursuing the most radical architectural departure in humanoid robotics: a musculoskeletal design with fluid-powered artificial muscles (Myofibers) replicating human anatomical structure with 206 degrees of freedom. Though far from commercial deployment, Clone has attracted a global community of robotics researchers and engineers drawn to its open development philosophy. If Myofiber technology can be manufactured at scale, it represents a fundamentally different cost and capability curve than conventional motor-gearbox designs.

name: UBTECH Robotics
description: UBTECH is China's largest humanoid robotics company by headcount and one of the longest-standing, having commercialized educational and entertainment humanoids since 2012. The Walker program, now on its X generation, represents over five years of commercial service deployments in hotel, banking, and retail environments. UBTECH's role in co-founding the Beijing Humanoid Robot Innovation Center and developing the Tiangong platform underscores its position as a key instrument of Chinese national humanoid strategy.

name: Xiaomi Robotics
description: Xiaomi's robotics division operates within the consumer electronics giant's broader innovation portfolio, with CyberOne as its flagship humanoid prototype. Xiaomi's strategic rationale mirrors Samsung and LG's forays into robotics: leveraging manufacturing scale, supply chain relationships, and brand recognition to enter the emerging home robot market. While CyberOne remains a prototype with limited capabilities, Xiaomi's manufacturing infrastructure and $9B+ cash position give it credible resources to accelerate if the consumer humanoid market matures.

name: Astribot (Stardust Intelligence)
description: Astribot, the commercial entity of Stardust Intelligence, has distinguished itself with viral demonstrations of high-speed household manipulation — its S1 robot's calligraphy, cooking, and laundry demonstrations achieved tens of millions of views in 2024. Backed by Tencent, Astribot is targeting the consumer and service robot market with a premium product positioned for hospitality and eventually home use. The company's speed-focused design philosophy — prioritizing end-effector velocity over brute payload — reflects a bet that consumer use cases value dexterity and speed over industrial-grade force.

name: Physical Intelligence (π)
description: Physical Intelligence (π) is not a robot hardware company but the leading foundation model lab for physical AI, developing general-purpose robot learning systems that can be deployed across diverse hardware platforms. Co-founded by Sergey Levine, Chelsea Finn, and Karol Hausman — three of the world's most cited robotics researchers — π's π0 and π0.2 vision-language-action models have achieved unprecedented zero-shot generalization across robot morphologies. Its approach of treating robot learning as a foundation model problem, rather than a per-robot-per-task engineering challenge, has drawn $470M+ and a $2.4B valuation despite selling no hardware.

name: Galbot
description: Galbot is Beijing's fastest-growing humanoid startup, having raised RMB 2.5B (~$345M) in a 2026 Series B led by Alibaba, Meituan, and Sequoia China — one of the largest humanoid raises in Chinese history. The company differentiates on full-body dexterous manipulation, with its G1 platform demonstrating class-leading task completion in logistics and precision assembly. Alibaba's lead position opens a potential deployment channel across its vast domestic logistics network, while Meituan's participation hints at last-mile delivery applications. Galbot's data collection infrastructure and manipulation-first architecture position it as China's answer to Physical Intelligence.

name: Sunday Robotics
description: Sunday Robotics achieved unicorn status in early 2026 with a $200M Series B at a $1.15B valuation, backed by Hillhouse Capital and GGV Capital. The company has made a deliberate pivot from pure-industrial to consumer-plus-commercial, targeting household assistance, elderly care, and domestic service in Japan, South Korea, and China — markets with acute demographic pressure and proven consumer willingness to pay for domestic help. Sunday's bet on the consumer humanoid market differentiates it from the factory-automation focus of most Chinese peers.

name: Mind Robotics
description: Mind Robotics was founded in early 2026 by RJ Scaringe, CEO of electric vehicle maker Rivian, who is running both companies concurrently. Mind Robotics is pursuing a contrarian philosophy: that current humanoid hardware is already sufficient for a broad range of applications, and the true bottleneck is cognitive architecture. Scaringe argues the industry has over-invested in hardware performance while under-investing in the reasoning and world-model capabilities that would make robots genuinely useful. The company is in stealth mode with an undisclosed seed round from investors including Rivian board members.

name: Mentee Robotics
description: Mentee Robotics was co-founded by Amnon Shashua, CEO of Mobileye, applying autonomous vehicle-grade perception and safety architecture to humanoid robotics. The company's MenteeBo platform integrates Mobileye's EyeQ chips and Responsibility-Sensitive Safety (RSS) framework, bringing AV-proven hardware to the humanoid space. Shashua's thesis is that the robotics industry will converge on the same sensor fusion, prediction, and planning stacks that have matured in automotive — and Mentee is positioned to license this technology stack rather than purely sell hardware.

name: Leju Robotics
description: Leju Robotics is a Shenzhen-based humanoid startup known for the Kuavo bipedal robot platform, designed as an accessible open-development system for research institutions and developers. Leju differentiates through its open SDK, competitive pricing, and a developer-community-first approach that has attracted adoption across Chinese and Southeast Asian universities. The Kuavo's agile locomotion, capable of dynamic walking and stair climbing, makes it a popular base platform for AI and manipulation research.

name: Skild AI
description: Skild AI, founded by CMU robotics professors Deepak Pathak and Abhinav Gupta, is developing a universal robot brain — a single foundation model capable of controlling diverse robot embodiments across manipulation, locomotion, and navigation tasks simultaneously. The company raised $300M in a landmark 2024 round at a $1.5B valuation before shipping a product, reflecting investor conviction in the general-purpose robot intelligence thesis. Skild's approach emphasizes world model pre-training on internet-scale data combined with robot-specific fine-tuning, positioning it as an infrastructure layer for the entire humanoid industry.
